summary: DHH details 37signals' plan to save approximately $7 million over five years by leaving the cloud. After spending $3.2 million on cloud services in 2022, they're purchasing around $600,000 in Dell hardware with AMD Zen4 chips to replace $2.3 million in annual cloud compute costs. The math works out to roughly $840,000 per year for owned hardware, power, and bandwidth versus $2.3 million in the cloud. He argues any mid-sized SaaS company with stable workloads that isn't benchmarking cloud costs against owned hardware is committing financial malpractice.
key_points: 37signals spent $3.2M on cloud in 2022, with $2.3M on compute they plan to replace with ~$600K in hardware amortized over five years
The total annual cost of owned infrastructure (hardware, power, bandwidth) comes to $840K versus $2.3M in the cloud
They're adding 4,000 vCPUs across two data centers using AMD Zen4 chips with Gen 4 NVMe drives, fitting into existing rack space
The cloud exit excludes S3 storage (8 petabytes) which will be addressed separately in 2024
They abandoned enterprise Kubernetes in favor of building their own tooling
money_quotes: We stand to save about $7m in server expenses over five years from doing so. Without changing the size of our ops team.
Spending $600,000 on a bunch of hardware might sound like a lot in the age of cloud. But if you amortize that over a conservative five years, it's just $120,000 per year!

Any mid-sized SaaS business and above with stable work loads that does not benchmark their rental bill for servers in the cloud against buying their own boxes is committing financial malpractice at this point.
That's a total of $840,000/year for everything. Bandwidth, power, and boxes on an amortization schedule of five years. Compared to $2.3m in the cloud.
In round numbers, let's call it saving a million and a half dollars per year.
I suggest you call Dell, then call Deft. Get some real world numbers. Make up your own mind.
key_insight: For mid-sized SaaS companies with stable workloads, owning hardware instead of renting cloud compute can cut server costs by more than 60%, making cloud-only strategies a form of financial malpractice.
